stem: In a advertising study comparing Platform A vs. Platform B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher conversion rate (70.0% vs 65.0%). However, when stratified by product category: In low-consideration products (n_X=40, n_Y=120): Treatment X achieves 80% vs Treatment Y's 74%. In high-consideration products (n_X=80, n_Y=20): Treatment X achieves 57% vs Treatment Y's 47%. The true population proportion of low-consideration products is uncertain, estimated between 31% and 51%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to high-consideration products (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.079, 0.087], point estimate: 0.083. Treatment X is genuinely better by 8.3 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=65.0%, Y=70.0% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In low-consideration products: X=80% > Y=74%. In high-consideration products: X=57% > Y=47%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Platform A is applied more to high-consideration products (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
